1: David’s consultation with ‘commanders of thousands and hundreds’ reflects the traditional Israelite military-administrative structure. This opening act of consultation (niwwāʿēṣ) distinguishes David from Saul, who unilaterally handled sacred matters without proper consultation. The Chronicler adds this council scene to 2 Sam 6’s account, emphasizing that the ark project began as a communal decision, not royal caprice.
2: ‘If it seems good to you (ʿālêkem ṭôb)’ — a conditional formula seeking community consensus. ‘The priests and Levites in their cities with pasturelands’ acknowledges the structure established in Joshua 21: Levites had designated cities throughout Israel (48 cities total) rather than a tribal territory. This reference situates the ark project within the proper Levitical framework even before it begins.
3: ‘We did not inquire of it (dĕrašnûhû) in the days of Saul’ — dāraš (seek, inquire) is the Chronicler’s most important theological term. The entire narrative of Chronicles is structured around the principle that seeking YHWH produces blessing, while failing to seek him produces disaster. Saul’s failure to inquire was explicitly his fatal sin (1 Chronicles 10:14). David frames the ark project as a correction of Saul’s neglect.
4: ‘The thing seemed right (kî-yāšar) in the eyes of all the people’ — the assembly ratifies David’s proposal. The near-universal agreement (‘all the assembly’) is characteristic of the Chronicler’s idealized portrayals of Israel’s great moments: the whole community participates in acts of worship and covenant renewal. This pattern mirrors the later temple dedication under Solomon.
5: ‘From Shihor of Egypt to the entrance of Hamath (lĕbôʾ ḥămāt)’ describes the ideal maximum extent of the land. The Shihor was an eastern branch of the Nile delta; Lebo-hamath was the northern boundary of the Promised Land (Numbers 13:21; 1 Chronicles 34:8; Joshua 13:5). This formulation signals that David’s gathering is pan-Israelite in its ideal scope — not the fractured monarchy of later years.
6: Kiriath-jearim (‘City of Forests’), also called Baalah (originally a Canaanite site), was in Judah near the Philistine border. The ark had rested there since the Philistines returned it after the plagues of 1 Samuel 5–6 (1 Samuel 7:1-2 mentions twenty years). Kiriath-jearim was one of four Gibeonite cities (Joshua 9:17) — a non-Israelite city hosting the ark underscores the irregular situation David sought to correct.
7: The ‘new cart’ (ʿăgālâ ḥădāšâ) is an inadvertent copying of Philistine practice. When the Philistines returned the ark, they placed it on a new cart drawn by two cows (1 Samuel 6:7-8). That method worked for the Philistines — but the Torah prescribed that the Kohathite Levites carry the ark on their shoulders using poles (Numbers 4:15; 1 Chronicles 7:9); no cart was permitted. Israel unknowingly adopted the pagan transport method and would pay for it at Chidon.
8: ‘David and all Israel were celebrating before God with all their might (bĕkol-ʿōz)’ — the festive language parallels 2 Samuel 6:5. The celebration was genuine but uninformed: devotion does not substitute for obedience to the prescribed form. The Chronicler presents this as a cautionary tale about well-intentioned but improperly ordered worship — a concern central to post-exilic restoration debates about proper temple practice.
9: The threshing floor of Chidon (called Nacon in 2 Samuel 6:6 — a textual variant between the two accounts, possibly different local names for the same site). Threshing floors were elevated, exposed platforms on hilltops; the cart’s stumble on such terrain would be expected. The incidental geographic detail grounds the narrative in physical reality while the theological interpretation (the ark’s holiness, not cart malfunction) frames the divine response.
10: ‘The anger of the LORD was kindled (wayyiḥar-ʾap yhwh)’ — the idiom ḥārâ ʾap (lit. ‘the nose burned’) is the standard Hebrew expression for intense anger. Uzza touched the ark ‘because the oxen stumbled (šāmaṭ)’ — his intention was protective. Yet Numbers 4:15 explicitly warned: ‘they must not touch the holy things or they will die.’ Holiness in the ANE was dangerous; the boundary between sacred and common operated as an objective category, not merely a matter of intention.
11: The site name Pereṣ-ʿuzzāʾ (‘Breach of Uzza’) preserves the event. Pereṣ (breach, breakout) is the same root as the name Perez (Genesis 38:29, ancestor of David via Judah). The geographic etiology records that divine holiness broke out against an unauthorized approach. Notably, the same verb (pāraṣ) will appear in 1 Chronicles 14:11 (Baal-perazim) where YHWH breaks out against Israel’s enemies — both uses are about divine irresistible force; the difference is the direction.
12: David’s fear is theologically appropriate. ‘How can I bring the ark of God to me?’ expresses the proper question: ark custody is a divine prerogative, not a royal one. The question implicitly acknowledges that proper procedure must be established before proceeding. The Chronicler uses this pause (ark at Obed-edom for three months) as a narrative breathing space for David to learn the correct Levitical protocol — which he then applies in chapter 15.
13: Obed-edom the Gittite: the designation ‘Gittite’ has puzzled interpreters. In 1 Chronicles 15:18, 24, Obed-edom appears as a Levite in the gatekeeper role. One solution: ‘Gittite’ means from Gath-rimmon, a Levitical city in Dan (Joshua 21:24-25), not Philistine Gath. If so, Obed-edom was a Levite of appropriate lineage — meaning the ark landed with the right kind of custodian, just in an improvised setting rather than the appointed location.
14: Three months of blessing on Obed-edom’s household establishes a narrative pattern: the ark’s presence brings blessing to those who host it properly. The same pattern appears with Laban when Jacob was with him (Genesis 30:27) and with Potiphar’s household when Joseph served him (Genesis 39:5). This blessing-through-the-bearer motif prepares for the permanent ark installation in Jerusalem: the whole city and kingdom will be blessed when the ark comes properly home.
